c586068b-360a-4125-8610-75754d31c801.title:
Find three different scientific studies or research papers that reached contradictory conclusions about the same topic (e.g., coffee and health, screen time effects, exercise frequency). For each study, identify: the research question, methodology, sample size, key findings, and potential limitations. Then analyze why the studies reached different conclusions, evaluate which methodology is most robust, and synthesize what the current scientific consensus is (if any). Cite all papers with URLs or DOIs.

Benchmark rule: when external evidence is needed, run search then visit the best URLs. Visit pages to extract concrete details and cite visited URLs in the final answer.

details.mandate:
Find three different scientific studies or research papers that reached contradictory conclusions about the same topic (e.g., coffee and health, screen time effects, exercise frequency). For each study, identify: the research question, methodology, sample size, key findings, and potential limitations. Then analyze why the studies reached different conclusions, evaluate which methodology is most robust, and synthesize what the current scientific consensus is (if any). Cite all papers with URLs or DOIs.

Benchmark rule: when external evidence is needed, run search then visit the best URLs. Visit pages to extract concrete details and cite visited URLs in the final answer.

8bdeaefb-bdb9-42c0-ba66-73e22e0d02ea.title: Sequential plan to find 3 contradictory studies on one topic, extract details, analyze differences, evaluate methods, and synthesize consensus

internal_plan.goal: Find three scientific studies/papers that reached contradictory conclusions about the same topic, extract for each: research question, methodology, sample size, key findings, and limitations; analyze why conclusions differ; evaluate which methodology is most robust; synthesize scientific consensus (if any); cite all papers with URLs/DOIs.
target_facts_to_extract: Exact research question or objective stated by each paper
Study design (randomized trial, cohort, case-control, cross-sectional, meta-analysis, etc.)
Sample size and key participant characteristics
Primary outcome(s) and main numerical results (e.g., risk ratios, hazard ratios, p-values, CIs)
Authors' stated limitations and any observable methodological weaknesses (e.g., confounding, measurement error, selection bias, short follow-up)
DOI or stable URL for citation
Systematic reviews/meta-analyses or guidelines relevant to topic to determine consensus
likely_sources: PubMed / NCBI (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov)
Journal publisher pages (NEJM, JAMA, BMJ, Lancet, PLOS, etc.)
DOI resolver (doi.org)
Google Scholar (scholar.google.com)
PubMed Central for full-text when available
Preprint servers if relevant (medRxiv, bioRxiv) but prefer peer-reviewed
Relevant meta-analyses and evidence syntheses (Cochrane Reviews, major meta-analyses)
verification_steps: Use search queries to identify candidate contradictory studies and record the source snippet
Visit each paper's webpage (publisher or PMC) to read full methods and results (visit required per benchmark rule)
Extract exact sample size and primary numerical results from methods/results sections, not from press releases or abstracts only
Cross-check DOI/URL and check publication type (peer-reviewed vs preprint)
Search for and visit one or two recent systematic reviews / meta-analyses on the topic to place findings in context
Note industry funding or conflicts of interest disclosed in papers
success_criteria (measurable): Identify exactly 3 primary research papers on a single topic with clearly contradictory conclusions (e.g., one finds benefit, one finds harm, one finds null effect OR sufficiently different effect estimates)
For each paper, provide the five required fields (research question, methodology, sample size, key findings with numerical results, limitations) with citations (DOI/URL)
Provide a reasoned analysis of why studies differ (at least 3 specific methodological or contextual reasons supported by extracted facts)
Provide assessment of which methodology is most robust with justification (reference to risk of bias, design strength, sample size, control of confounding)
Cite at least one systematic review/meta-analysis or guideline that addresses the consensus and include its URL/DOI

subproblems.title: Select a topic that has documented contradictory findings in the literature
subproblems.intent: Decide on a single topic where multiple high-quality papers report contradictory conclusions (candidate topics: coffee and mortality/health; screen time and children's cognitive outcomes; exercise frequency and mortality/cardiovascular risk).

expected_output_fields.rationale_for_choice: string explaining why topic likely has contradictory studies and available sources

likely_sources: High-level reviews or news summaries noting controversy (BMJ opinion pieces, PubMed reviews)
verification_steps: Quick literature scan (searches in next subproblem) to confirm presence of contradictory primary studies
subproblems.why_reduces_uncertainty: Narrowing to one topic focuses downstream searches and ensures the studies compared address the same question or closely related questions.

subproblems.title: Search for candidate primary studies with contradictory conclusions on the chosen topic
subproblems.intent: Find at least 6 candidate papers (so we can select 3 that directly contradict each other) including their titles, short snippets, and URLs/DOIs. Prioritize peer-reviewed original research articles with accessible full text.
queries: topic + study shows benefit (e.g., 'coffee and mortality cohort study reduced risk DOI')
topic + study shows harm (e.g., 'coffee increases risk atrial fibrillation cohort DOI')
topic + null/no association (e.g., 'coffee no association mortality cohort DOI')

expected_output_fields.candidate_papers: list of {title, authors, year, source (journal), short snippet, url/DOI, claimed conclusion (benefit/harm/null)}

likely_sources: PubMed search results
Google Scholar
Journal webpages
verification_steps: Ensure results include at least one paper representing each conclusion (positive, negative, null).
Prioritize papers with accessible full text for visiting step.
subproblems.why_reduces_uncertainty: Provides raw candidate papers to inspect; searching finds URLs which must be visited to extract precise methodological details.

subproblems.title: Visit the selected primary study webpages to extract required details
subproblems.intent: Open the full text or publisher abstract pages for each chosen paper and extract: research question, full methodology (design, exposures, outcomes, statistical methods), exact sample size, key numerical results (effect sizes, CIs, p-values), funding/conflicts, and stated limitations.
likely_sources: Publisher article page (Nature, JAMA, BMJ, Circulation, etc.)
PubMed Central full text when available
verification_steps: Read methods and results sections directly, not only abstracts
If full text behind paywall, use PubMed abstract plus DOI to check supplementary materials or open preprints
Record the exact quote or table cell for sample size and primary numerical results
subproblems.why_reduces_uncertainty: Visiting provides authoritative methodological details needed to evaluate why studies disagree and to compute comparability across studies.

subproblems.title: Search for and visit one or more recent systematic reviews / meta-analyses and professional guidelines on the topic
subproblems.intent: Identify and read at least one recent (last 5–10 years) systematic review or meta-analysis that synthesizes the literature on the chosen topic and any relevant professional guideline or statement to determine consensus.
queries: topic + 'systematic review' OR 'meta-analysis' OR 'Cochrane' OR 'guideline' + DOI

expected_output_fields.main_conclusion_of_review: string summary and numerical pooled effect if available

likely_sources: Cochrane Library, PubMed, BMJ, JAMA, Lancet, major specialty society guidelines
verification_steps: Visit review full text and extract pooled estimates, heterogeneity metrics (I^2), and authors' interpretation
Check date to ensure it includes the primary studies selected or note omission
subproblems.why_reduces_uncertainty: Systematic reviews aggregate evidence and explicitly address heterogeneity; they help determine whether apparent contradictions are resolved at the synthesis level.

subproblems.title: Compare and analyze why the three selected primary studies reached different conclusions
subproblems.intent: Using the extracted structured data, identify and document the most plausible methodological/contexts reasons for differing results (e.g., different exposure definitions, confounding control, sample populations, follow-up duration, statistical power).

expected_output_fields.comparison_table: structured differences across studies (design, exposure, outcome, covariates adjusted, sample differences)
expected_output_fields.top_reasons_for_discrepancy: list with evidence linking to extracted facts

verification_steps: Cite specific passages (methods/results) from visited pages to support each claimed difference
subproblems.why_reduces_uncertainty: Moves from raw facts to causal explanations for discrepant findings; necessary before judging methodological robustness.

subproblems.title: Evaluate which methodology is most robust and synthesize current scientific consensus
subproblems.intent: Assess risk of bias for each study (using principles: randomized > prospective cohort > case-control > cross-sectional; large sample, pre-registered analysis, thorough confounder control, objective outcomes stronger) and combine with systematic review conclusions to state consensus.

expected_output_fields.methodological_ranking: ordered list of the three studies with justification
expected_output_fields.consensus_statement: short paragraph describing current consensus or lack thereof, citing systematic review/guideline

verification_steps: Reference extracted limitations, sample sizes, and adjuster lists to justify ranking
Use review/meta-analysis conclusions to support consensus statement
subproblems.why_reduces_uncertainty: Final assessment explains which evidence should be weighted more when forming conclusions and whether a robust consensus exists.

subproblems.title: Prepare final deliverable: structured report with extracted fields, comparative analysis, methodology evaluation, and citations (URLs/DOIs)
subproblems.intent: Assemble all extracted data and analyses into a final, citable report that includes direct links/DOIs to each paper and the review, and a concise synthesis.

expected_output_fields.final_report: text containing for each paper: research question, methodology, sample size, key findings (numeric), limitations, DOI/URL; plus analysis and consensus synthesis

verification_steps: Ensure every factual claim about a paper cites the visited URL/DOI
Double-check numbers (sample sizes and effect estimates) against visited pages
subproblems.why_reduces_uncertainty: Produces the artifact requested by the user with verifiable citations and structured detail.
